platforms.name: Steam

config_files.path_note: Path uses the Steam default library. Replace the Steam library root if the user has a custom library location. The 'dota 2 beta' folder name is the actual install folder name despite the game being fully released.
config_files.notes: Dota 2 uses the same Source 2 video.txt format as CS2. Overwritten by the in-game Video settings menu. Edit only while Dota 2 is fully closed.
Executed on game start. Create this file if it does not exist.

platforms.launch_options_path: Steam Library > Right-click Dota 2 > Properties > Launch Options

keys.notes: Exclusive fullscreen provides lower latency. Borderless is useful for streamers or multi-monitor use.

keys.effect: Texture quality / GPU memory usage level: 0=low, 1=medium, 2=high.

console_vars.notes: Same recommendation as CS2 — cap 10 above monitor Hz to prevent thermal overhead without impacting smoothness.

console_vars.effect: Delay in seconds before the game-over screen appears. Default 5.
console_vars.notes: Quality-of-life setting; not a performance key. Setting to 0 skips the end-game delay.

launch_options.effect: Pre-loads the main Dota 2 map on startup, reducing in-game load times.

notes: Dota 2 uses the same Source 2 video.txt format as CS2. The same engine profile (source-2.json) applies to both games.
The Dota 2 install folder is named 'dota 2 beta' despite the game being fully released since 2013. This is a common source of confusion when locating config files.
Dota 2 is predominantly CPU-limited, particularly at high player action rates (teamfights, mass unit control). Lowering cpu_level is effective.
Dota 2's renderer supports Vulkan via the -vulkan launch option. On some AMD and Intel systems, Vulkan provides better performance than DX11. Test per-system.
